INSURANCE AGENTS · Oklahoma City, OK · Monday, July 20, 2026
Building Momentum

Housing starts surge 19% while permit decline signals uneven construction rebound

Privately-owned housing starts hit a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1.427 million in June 2026, up 19.0% month-over-month and 3.5% year-over-year, according to the Census Bureau. Building permits, however, declined 3.0% m/m to 1.367 million and fell 2.3% y/y, with single-family permits down 2.4%, suggesting divergent momentum in the residential construction pipeline. Completions advanced 3.3% m/m to 1.392 million, with single-family completions up 6.6%.

The headline gains mask a concentration risk: single-family starts remained essentially flat at 895,000 units (down 0.2%), while the surge was driven by multifamily construction, which jumped to 513,000 units in the 5+ unit category. This split reflects broader uncertainty about consumer demand even as the raw construction numbers tick higher.

The divergence between starts and permits raises a near-term question for the trades: whether June’s bounce is sustainable or reflects the completion of projects already in the pipeline before the slowdown took hold.

Market Signals
Sentiment Check

Builder confidence drops to 34 as affordability pressures persist despite strong starts

The NAHB/Wells Fargo Housing Market Index fell to 34 in July 2026, down two points from a revised 36 in June, according to the National Association of Home Builders. This marks the 15th consecutive month below the 40 threshold—the longest such stretch since 2012. All three index components weakened: current sales conditions to 37, six-month sales expectations to 43, and buyer traffic to 23.

Builders are responding with aggressive pricing: 37% cut prices in July (up from 35% in June), averaging a 6% reduction, while 63% deployed sales incentives for the 16th straight month. Regional variation is stark, with the West at 26 and the South at 33, versus the Northeast at 45 and Midwest at 45. NAHB Chief Economist Robert Dietz characterized affordability as “the home building industry’s primary challenge,” while Chairman Bill Owens noted that “many potential buyers remain on the sidelines.”

Item of Interest
Infrastructure Alert

Burst 36-inch water main floods West Hollywood, opens sidewalk sinkhole

A 36-inch steel trunk water line installed in 1916 ruptured Thursday, July 16, 2026, at Sunset Boulevard and Holloway Drive in West Hollywood around 2:30 a.m., according to ABC7 LA. The break sent rushing water through nearby streets for roughly four hours before crews stopped the flow around 7 a.m., opening a sidewalk sinkhole that trapped two men (both uninjured). The floodwater inundated dozens of apartment-building garages and parked cars along Palm Avenue and reached a Metro bus yard on Santa Monica Boulevard, partially submerging several buses. The LADWP has begun long-term repairs on the aging line.

The Insurance Agents Spotlight
Claims & Litigation

Connecticut regulators champion FORTIFIED roof standard amid rising severe weather frequency

Connecticut Insurance Commissioner Josh Hershman and Assistant Deputy Commissioner George Bradner promoted the IBHS FORTIFIED Roof standard to builders, remodelers, and roofers at a July event hosted by the Home Builders and Remodelers Association of the Connecticut River Region, according to Insurance Business America. Hershman framed FORTIFIED standards as offering “a framework for strengthening properties,” while Bradner emphasized that proactive mitigation is essential as severe weather becomes more frequent. Connecticut has not mandated FORTIFIED premium discounts—unlike Alabama and Kentucky, which pair the standard with required wind-mitigation discounts of 20-55% (Kentucky’s mandate took effect March 2026). Nationally, FORTIFIED designations have surpassed 100,000 across 34 states by May 2026, with Alabama leading at 53,000+ designations under its Strengthen Alabama Homes program, which has distributed $86M+ in grants since 2016.
